request.question: Are Jon Fratelli and Skye Edwards from the same country?

title: Jon Fratelli
Morcheeba
Skye Edwards
The Fratellis
Codeine Velvet Club
Chelsea Dagger
Charles Reeves
Eyes Wide, Tongue Tied
A Heady Tale
Psycho Jukebox
sentences: Jon Fratelli (born John Paul Lawler, 4 March 1979, Glasgow, Scotland) is a Scottish musician and songwriter best known for his work with the band The Fratellis.
He has also played in a band called Codeine Velvet Club, and also performed as a solo artist.
Morcheeba are an English electronic band formed in the mid 1990s with founding members vocalist Skye Edwards and the brothers Paul and Ross Godfrey.
They mix influences from trip hop, rock, folk-rock and downtempo, and have produced eight regular studio albums since 1995, two of which reached the UK top ten.
Their last studio album, "Head Up High", was released in October 2013.
Edwards left the band in 2003, after which the brothers used a number of singers before she rejoined in 2009.
They recruit additional members for their live performances and have toured internationally.
In 2014 Paul Godfrey resigned from the band, thus ending Morcheeba.
Edwards and Ross Godfrey later formed Skye|Ross and released a self-titled album in September 2016.
They continue to tour, on occasion being listed as "Morcheeba / Sky & Ross Trio".
Skye Edwards (born Shirley Klaris Yonavieve Edwards; 27 May 1974) is a British singer-songwriter.
Her career began in 1994 when she and the Godfrey brothers (Paul Godfrey, a DJ, and Ross Godfrey, a multiinstrumentalist) formed Morcheeba, which released five albums with Skye as lead vocalist.
In 2003, the band split, after which Skye released two solo albums: "Mind How You Go" in 2006, and "Keeping Secrets" in 2009.
In 2010, Edwards returned to Morcheeba, again as lead vocalist.
In 2012, she released her third solo album, "Back to Now".
Her fourth solo album "In A Low Light" was released in 2015.
The Fratellis are a Scottish rock band from Glasgow, formed in 2005.

The band consists of lead vocalist and guitarist Jon Fratelli (born John Lawler), bass guitarist Barry Fratelli (born Barry Wallace), and drummer and backing vocalist Mince Fratelli (born Gordon McRory).
Their singles "Chelsea Dagger" and "Whistle for the Choir" were both top ten hits in the UK charts.
Codeine Velvet Club was a Scottish alternative rock band formed in 2008 by Lou Hickey and Jon Lawler, a.k.a. Jon Fratelli from The Fratellis.
The band's debut album "Codeine Velvet Club" was released on 28 December 2009 in the UK and on 6 April 2010 in the US.
"Chelsea Dagger" is a song by Scottish rock band The Fratellis.
It was released as the second single from their debut studio album, "Costello Music" (2006), on 28 August 2006.
It is named after Jon Fratelli's wife Heather, a burlesque dancer whose stage name – a play on Britney Spears – he borrowed for the song.
Fratelli described the tune as "a rock 'n' roll gig in an old speakeasy or something like that."

Charles "Chicky" Reeves is a producer, recording, mixing and mastering engineer working with famous artists such as Prince, UB40, Ray Charles, Tito Puente, Johnny Cash, and Radiohead—as well as MTV's Live at the Ten Spot, VH1 Storytellers, Boyz II Men, Apollo Heights, D-Lite, 247Spys.
In 2004, Reeves moved to London with his New-York-schooled-style.

He continues to collaborate with artists in the studio as well as mixing front of house for Orchestral Manoeuvres in the Dark, Grace Jones, Francesca Longrigg, Scarlett Etienne, Dana Boulé, BEF (formerly Heaven 17), Onetwo, Skye Edwards (of Morcheeba), Prince, Rachael Cormack, UB40, and the Icelandic band Trabant.
His studio, Sublime Studios, is located in Islington, London, England.
His music is published by Extreme Music.
Eyes Wide, Tongue Tied is the fourth album by Scottish rock band The Fratellis.
The album was revealed on 1 June 2015, alongside the first track "Me and the Devil" (which was given away for free from the bands website), and released on 21 August 2015.

The album was written and recorded in Los Angeles, California during November 2014 with Tony Hoffer, who also produced the band's debut album "Costello Music" and Jon Fratelli's solo album "Psycho Jukebox".
"A Heady Tale" is the third single by Scottish rock band, The Fratellis from their second album, "Here We Stand".
It was released on 22 December 2008.
This song is 1 of the 4 tracks on "Here We Stand" to have a heavy use of piano (the other 3 being "Mistress Mabel", "Milk And Money" & "Moriaty's Last Stand").
The B-sides "Mon Yous, Mon us but no' Them" and "When All the Lights Go Out" were also piano intensive.
Jon Fratelli has said that the B-Side "Lonesome Anti Christmas Blues" is the "most depressing Christmas song written".
Psycho Jukebox is the debut solo album by Jon Fratelli, frontman of The Fratellis and formerly of Codeine Velvet Club.
Released on 25 July 2011.
It was recorded with Tony Hoffer (who produced "Costello Music" and mixed "Codeine Velvet Club") in the Sound Factory studios in Los Angeles.

candidate.summarize1.predict:

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

response.hop1_query: Are Jon Fratelli and Skye Edwards from the same country?
hop1_docs: The Fratellis | The Fratellis are a Scottish rock band from Glasgow, formed in 2005.  The band consists of lead vocalist and guitarist Jon Fratelli (born John Lawler), bass guitarist Barry Fratelli (born Barry Wallace), and drummer and backing vocalist Mince Fratelli (born Gordon McRory).  Their singles "Chelsea Dagger" and "Whistle for the Choir" were both top ten hits in the UK charts.

Codeine Velvet Club | Codeine Velvet Club was a Scottish alternative rock band formed in 2008 by Lou Hickey and Jon Lawler, a.k.a. Jon Fratelli from The Fratellis.  The band's debut album "Codeine Velvet Club" was released on 28 December 2009 in the UK and on 6 April 2010 in the US.

Jon Fratelli | Jon Fratelli (born John Paul Lawler, 4 March 1979, Glasgow, Scotland) is a Scottish musician and songwriter best known for his work with the band The Fratellis.  He has also played in a band called Codeine Velvet Club, and also performed as a solo artist.

Chelsea Dagger | "Chelsea Dagger" is a song by Scottish rock band The Fratellis.  It was released as the second single from their debut studio album, "Costello Music" (2006), on 28 August 2006.  It is named after Jon Fratelli's wife Heather, a burlesque dancer whose stage name – a play on Britney Spears – he borrowed for the song.  Fratelli described the tune as "a rock 'n' roll gig in an old speakeasy or something like that."

Skye-Jilly Edwards | Skye-Jilly Edwards (born c. 1972) is an Australian beauty pageant titleholder who won the Miss Globe International 1994 in Istanbul, Turkey and represented her country in the Miss World 1994 pageant in Sun City, South Africa.  She was rated in the top 50 models in Australia by Ralph Magazine.

Psycho Jukebox | Psycho Jukebox is the debut solo album by Jon Fratelli, frontman of The Fratellis and formerly of Codeine Velvet Club.  Released on 25 July 2011.  It was recorded with Tony Hoffer (who produced "Costello Music" and mixed "Codeine Velvet Club") in the Sound Factory studios in Los Angeles.

The Skye Boat Song | "The Skye Boat Song" is a Scottish folk song, which can be played as a waltz, recalling the escape of Prince Charles Edward Stuart (Bonnie Prince Charlie) from Uist to the Isle of Skye after his defeat at the Battle of Culloden in 1746.

Primary topics: Jon Fratelli (The Fratellis) and Skye-Jilly Edwards (pageant winner); "Skye Edwards" is ambiguous.

- Jon Fratelli — "Jon Fratelli (born John Paul Lawler, 4 March 1979, Glasgow, Scotland)" and described as "a Scottish musician".
- Skye-Jilly Edwards — "Skye-Jilly Edwards (born c. 1972) is an Australian beauty pageant titleholder"; "won the Miss Globe International 1994" and "represented her country in the Miss World 1994".

Candidate entities: (A) Skye-Jilly Edwards (Australian) from the passages; (B) possibly a different "Skye Edwards" (not present in these passages). Missing fact to resolve the question: which "Skye Edwards" the user means and that person's nationality/birthplace (bridge entity: the name "Skye Edwards").
- Prioritized next-hop retrievals:
  1. "Skye Edwards" (Wikipedia page) — inspect lede/infobox for nationality/birthplace (to check if this is the Morcheeba singer).
  2. "Skye-Jilly Edwards" (Wikipedia page) — inspect lede/infobox to confirm Australian nationality.
  3. "Skye Edwards (disambiguation)" — check list of people named Skye Edwards and their nationalities/birthplaces.
  4. "Jon Fratelli" lede/infobox (confirm "Scottish", already shown in passages; low priority).
hop2_docs: Skye Edwards | Skye Edwards (born Shirley Klaris Yonavieve Edwards; 27 May 1974) is a British singer-songwriter.  Her career began in 1994 when she and the Godfrey brothers (Paul Godfrey, a DJ, and Ross Godfrey, a multiinstrumentalist) formed Morcheeba, which released five albums with Skye as lead vocalist.  In 2003, the band split, after which Skye released two solo albums: "Mind How You Go" in 2006, and "Keeping Secrets" in 2009.  In 2010, Edwards returned to Morcheeba, again as lead vocalist.  In 2012, she released her third solo album, "Back to Now".  Her fourth solo album "In A Low Light" was released in 2015.

Raasay | Raasay (Scottish Gaelic: "Ratharsair" ) is an island between the Isle of Skye and the mainland of Scotland.  It is separated from Skye by the Sound of Raasay and from Applecross by the Inner Sound.  It is most famous for being the birthplace of the poet Sorley MacLean, an important figure in the Scottish literary renaissance.

The Skye Boat Song | "The Skye Boat Song" is a Scottish folk song, which can be played as a waltz, recalling the escape of Prince Charles Edward Stuart (Bonnie Prince Charlie) from Uist to the Isle of Skye after his defeat at the Battle of Culloden in 1746.

Skye-Jilly Edwards | Skye-Jilly Edwards (born c. 1972) is an Australian beauty pageant titleholder who won the Miss Globe International 1994 in Istanbul, Turkey and represented her country in the Miss World 1994 pageant in Sun City, South Africa.  She was rated in the top 50 models in Australia by Ralph Magazine.

Mind How You Go (Skye Edwards album) | Mind How You Go is Skye Edwards's debut solo album, following her departure from Morcheeba.  Despite anticipation by fans, the album achieved limited success in the UK as a result of insufficient promotion.  It was more successful in other parts of Europe, particularly Poland, France, Italy and Switzerland.

Edward Hopper Birthplace and Boyhood Home | Edward Hopper Birthplace and Boyhood Home, also known as the Edward Hopper House Art Center, is an art center and historic home located at Nyack in Rockland County, New York.  It is a 2-story, side hall, mid-19th-century Greek Revival–style dwelling with a 2 ⁄ -story, Queen Anne–style addition.  It was the home of noted artist Edward Hopper (1882–1967) from the time of his birth until he moved to Manhattan in 1910.  He held title to the house until his death.

Skye Camanachd | Skye Camanachd is a shinty team from the Isle of Skye, Highland, Scotland.  It plays in the Premier Division and has a reserve team in North Division One, as well as a Ladies team in the WCA National Division One and a Ladies reserve team in the WCA Development League.  The club is based at Pairc nan Laoch, Portree.

response.summary_2: Jon Fratelli is Scottish; whether "Skye Edwards" is from the same country depends on which person you mean. Skye-Jilly Edwards is Australian. Skye Edwards is a British singer-songwriter.
response.answer:
I can’t give a single yes/no from the summaries because "Skye Edwards" is ambiguous.

- Jon Fratelli is described as Scottish.
- If you mean Skye-Jilly Edwards (per the summaries), she is Australian — so they are from different countries.
- If you mean Skye Edwards the British singer-songwriter (per the summaries), the summaries do not provide a clear, single-country match with "Scottish" (the name refers to different national descriptors).

Which Skye Edwards do you mean?
